prompt:
A NOTAM Q-line reads: Q) KZNY/QMRLC/IV/NBO/A/000/999/4038N07356W005

What subject and condition does the NOTAM code QMRLC describe?
gold_decision: Subject MR = Movement area (runway). Condition LC = Closed. The NOTAM code QMRLC indicates a runway closure at the specified aerodrome.
required_safety_constraints: NOTAM code subject is always characters 2-3 of the Q-code (MR = Movement area/Runway)
NOTAM code condition is always characters 4-5 of the Q-code (LC = Closed)
Traffic IV means the NOTAM applies to IFR and VFR traffic
acceptable_variants: QMRLC = runway closed (Movement area, Line Closed is also acceptable interpretation at some FIRs)
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix A — NOTAM Q-code subject/condition tables

provenance.generation_rule: Q-code constructed from FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix A subject code MR (Movement area/Runway) + condition code LC (Closed). FIR KZNY, coordinates correspond to JFK airport area. All sub-fields per ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 §6.2 table.

prompt:
A NOTAM C-field reads: C) 2601152359EST

What does the EST suffix signify, and what operational implication does it carry compared to a firm C-field time?
gold_decision: EST means the end time is an estimate, not a confirmed restoration time. Operationally, pilots and dispatchers must treat the restriction as potentially still active after 2601152359 UTC until a NOTAMN or NOTAMC confirms restoration. Operations should not be planned assuming the restriction is lifted at that exact time.
required_safety_constraints: EST suffix = estimated end time; restriction may continue beyond stated time (FAA JO 7930.2S §3-3-2)
PERM means the condition is considered permanent with no planned end date
A NOTAMC (cancellation) is the authoritative way to confirm a restriction is lifted
Planning operations that depend on an EST end time without confirming cancellation is non-compliant
evidence_requirements: ICAO Annex 15 §6.2.3 — Estimated end time interpretation

provenance.generation_rule: Date/time string constructed per FAA JO 7930.2S YYMMDDHHmm format: 26=year 2026, 01=Jan, 15=15th, 2359=23:59 UTC. EST suffix per JO 7930.2S §3-3-2. Knowledge point drawn from JO 7930.2S §3-3-2 and ICAO Annex 15 §6.2.3.

prompt:
NOTAM D) field contains: D) MON-FRI 0600-2200

A flight is planned to depart at 22:30 UTC on a Tuesday. Is the restriction in this NOTAM active at departure time?
gold_decision: No. The D-field schedule specifies the restriction is active Monday through Friday from 0600 to 2200 UTC. At 22:30 UTC on Tuesday, the schedule window has closed (2200 UTC is the end of the active period), so the restriction is NOT active at departure time.
required_safety_constraints: D-field times in NOTAMs are expressed in UTC unless explicitly stated otherwise (FAA JO 7930.2S §3-3-4)
The active period ends AT the stated time; operations AT 2200 must confirm whether the window is inclusive or exclusive per local ATC coordination
The B and C fields define the overall validity window; the D field sub-divides it
acceptable_variants: Some interpretations treat 2200 as the last minute of active restriction rather than the start of the inactive period — this ambiguity should be resolved with ATC for safety-critical operations

provenance.generation_rule: D-field schedule drawn from standard FAA JO 7930.2S schedule field format examples. UTC interpretation rule from JO 7930.2S §3-3-4 and ICAO Annex 15. Scenario crafted to probe boundary condition at exactly schedule end time.

prompt: What is the difference between a NOTAM N (New), NOTAM R (Replacement), and NOTAM C (Cancellation)? If NOTAM A1234/26 NOTAMR replaces A0987/26 NOTAMN, which NOTAM is operationally current?
gold_decision: NOTAM N creates a new notice. NOTAM R replaces a previous NOTAM — the prior NOTAM (A0987/26) is no longer valid and A1234/26 NOTAMR is the operative notice. NOTAM C cancels a previous NOTAM entirely. When A1234/26 NOTAMR is issued replacing A0987/26, pilots and dispatchers must use A1234/26 and discard A0987/26.
required_safety_constraints: A NOTAMR supersedes the referenced NOTAM; the original is no longer operationally valid
A NOTAMC cancels and removes the referenced NOTAM from the active NOTAM set
Using a superseded NOTAMN after a NOTAMR has been issued constitutes use of stale information (FailureMode: stale_information)
Replacement NOTAMs may change any field including effective times, scope, and text

provenance.generation_rule: Knowledge point from FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-3 NOTAM type designator table. Scenario uses notional NOTAM numbers formatted per standard YYYYseries/YY convention. NOTAMN/NOTAMR/NOTAMC distinction is a core NOTAM literacy test per proposal §7 Family 4 taxonomy.

prompt: A NOTAM Q-line scope field contains 'AE'. What does this scope designator mean, and what types of operations does it affect?
gold_decision: Scope AE means the NOTAM affects both Aerodrome (A) and En-route (E) operations. It applies to both aircraft on the ground/in the vicinity of the aerodrome and aircraft in transit through the associated airspace. Dispatchers and pilots filing IFR flight plans must check both A-scoped and E-scoped NOTAMs for affected airports and the en-route structure.
required_safety_constraints: Scope A = Aerodrome only
Scope E = En-route only
Scope W = Navigation warning (airspace, restricted areas)
Scope AE = both Aerodrome and En-route — cannot be ignored by either arrival/departure or en-route planning
ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 §6.2.6 defines all scope codes
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix A — Q-line scope field

provenance.generation_rule: Q-line scope codes derived from ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 §6.2.6 and FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix A. Scope AE is a frequent source of pilot/dispatcher misinterpretation per ASRS safety literature.

prompt:
NOTAM text (E field): 'RWY 13R/31L CLSD FOR MAINTENANCE.'

Is NOTAM runway closure a mandatory prohibition or an advisory? Under what regulation?
gold_decision: A runway closure NOTAM is a mandatory prohibition. Operations on a closed runway without explicit ATC authorization are a violation of 14 CFR Part 91 and FAA Order 7110.65 §3-7-2 (runway use procedures). Pilots must not use a closed runway even in an emergency without ATC authorization, and dispatchers must plan alternate departure runways or delay until the closure is lifted.
required_safety_constraints: Runway closure NOTAMs are MANDATORY, not advisory (14 CFR §91.129, FAA JO 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
Operating on a closed runway without ATC authorization is a potential runway incursion safety event
NOTAM purpose code N (Normal) or B (Briefing) does not downgrade mandatory status of a physical closure
If no alternate runway is available and closure prevents safe operations, dispatch must not release the flight
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §91.129 — Operations in Class D airspace runway requirements
FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-1 — NOTAM definition and applicability

provenance.generation_rule: NOTAM E-field text constructed in standard FAA contraction format per JO 7930.2S Appendix B (CLSD = closed, RWY = runway). Regulatory basis 14 CFR §91.129 and FAA JO 7110.65Z §3-7-2. Probe for advisory/mandatory confusion failure mode (FailureMode: advisory_mandatory_confusion).

prompt: What is the meaning of 'PERM' in a NOTAM C-field, and how does it differ from a long-term estimated end date?
gold_decision: PERM in the C-field means the condition described is considered permanent — there is no planned end date and no expectation of restoration. The NOTAM remains in the active NOTAM set indefinitely until a NOTAMC cancels it. A long-term EST date still implies intent to restore by that date, whereas PERM indicates the change is structural (e.g., permanent decommissioning of a navaid or permanent relocation of a threshold).
required_safety_constraints: PERM does not mean the NOTAM cannot be cancelled — a NOTAMC can cancel a PERM NOTAM
PERM conditions must be treated as permanent for flight planning purposes until explicitly cancelled
Confusing PERM with a long-term EST can lead to incorrect planning assumptions about facility availability
evidence_requirements: ICAO Annex 15 §6.2.3 — Permanent NOTAM end field

provenance.generation_rule: Knowledge point directly derived from FAA JO 7930.2S §3-3-2 definition of PERM in the C-field. Distinguishing PERM from long-term EST is a key disambiguation needed for dispatch planning.

prompt:
A NOTAM has the following Q-line purpose codes: Q) KZHU/QXXXX/IV/M/AE/000/999/...

The purpose code is 'M'. What does purpose code M mean and which operations must include this NOTAM in their briefing?
gold_decision: Purpose code M means the NOTAM is Military in nature — it was generated for military operations or airspace. Civilian operators in the affected area must still check M-coded NOTAMs if the Q-line scope and FIR includes their route. However, M-coded NOTAMs are typically of most relevance to military flights. Purpose codes: N=Normal, B=Briefing, O=Flight Operations, M=Military, K=Checklist.
required_safety_constraints: Purpose code M NOTAMs must still be checked by civilian operators if the scope affects their route or aerodrome
M-coded NOTAMs may restrict civilian operations in affected airspace (e.g. temporary MOA activation)
Purpose code B = mandatory for pilot briefing; code K = checklist only (not for operational briefing)
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix A — Q-line purpose field

provenance.generation_rule: Purpose code M from ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 §6.2.5 table. All purpose codes (N/B/O/M/K) directly from standard. QXXXX used as placeholder — actual subject/condition not relevant for this Q-line purpose code question.

prompt:
A NOTAM B-field reads: B) 2603011400
A NOTAM C-field reads: C) 2603022359

A flight departs at 14:30 UTC on 1 March 2026 and arrives at 09:00 UTC on 2 March 2026. Is the NOTAM applicable to the flight?
gold_decision: Yes. The NOTAM is active from 2026-03-01 14:00 UTC to 2026-03-02 23:59 UTC. The flight departs at 14:30 UTC on 1 March (after NOTAM activation) and arrives at 09:00 UTC on 2 March (before NOTAM expiry). The NOTAM is active throughout the entire flight duration and must be considered for both departure and arrival planning.
required_safety_constraints: NOTAM applicability must be checked against both departure and arrival times for en-route/aerodrome-scoped NOTAMs
B-field is effective start in UTC; C-field is effective end in UTC (FAA JO 7930.2S §3-3-1, §3-3-2)
A NOTAM that starts after departure but before arrival is still applicable to the operation

provenance.generation_rule: Date/time values constructed per FAA JO 7930.2S YYMMDDHHmm format. Scenario designed to probe full flight-envelope NOTAM applicability check — a common error is only checking departure-time validity.

prompt: Explain the difference between the NOTAM A-field (location indicator) and the Q-line FIR code. Can they differ, and what does it mean if they do?
gold_decision: The A-field contains the ICAO location indicator of the aerodrome or fix directly affected by the NOTAM (e.g., KJFK). The Q-line FIR code identifies the Flight Information Region responsible for issuing and maintaining the NOTAM (e.g., KZNY for New York Oceanic FIR). They can and do differ — for example, an airport NOTAM for KJFK will be managed by FIR KZNY. If they differ, the NOTAM still applies to the A-field location; the FIR code identifies administrative responsibility, not the affected area.
required_safety_constraints: A-field location is the operationally affected site — always use A-field for applicability determination
FIR code is administrative — it identifies which ATS unit issued the NOTAM
Confusing FIR with affected location can cause wrong-airport-applicability failures
evidence_requirements: ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 §6.2.1 — Q-line FIR field

provenance.generation_rule: Knowledge point from ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 distinction between Q-line FIR (administrative) and A-field location (operational). KJFK/KZNY example uses real FIR-airport relationship per FAA NFDC structure.

prompt:
A NOTAM E-field states: 'ILS RWY 28L LOC U/S'

What does U/S mean in FAA NOTAM contractions, and what approach types remain available for RWY 28L?
gold_decision: U/S is the FAA NOTAM contraction for 'unserviceable'. The ILS localizer for runway 28L is out of service. Instrument approaches to RWY 28L that require only the glideslope (GS) without the localizer are not available as a full ILS. Remaining options at minimum: VOR, RNAV (GPS), or LPV approach to 28L if published, or visual approaches. No ILS approaches (ILS/LOC/LOC BC) to RWY 28L until the LOC is restored.
required_safety_constraints: ILS requires both localizer (LOC) and glideslope (GS) — LOC U/S means no ILS approach is available
A 'LOC only' approach (lower minimums than full ILS) is also unavailable when LOC is U/S
Alternate approach procedures must be filed if ILS 28L was the planned approach and no other suitable instrument approach exists
U/S contraction = Unserviceable per FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix B
acceptable_variants: If a GS-only approach chart exists (unusual), it might be theoretically available, but this is operationally non-standard
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix B — NOTAM contraction U/S
14 CFR §91.175 — Instrument approach procedures and equipment requirements

provenance.generation_rule: NOTAM contraction U/S from FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix B contraction table. ILS localizer scenario is a canonical NOTAM training example. Knowledge point tests ability to infer downstream approach availability from equipment NOTAM.

prompt: A NOTAM reads: 'BIRD ACTIVITY VICINITY ARPT.' Is this NOTAM mandatory or advisory? How should a dispatching operation handle it?
gold_decision: Bird activity NOTAMs are advisory in nature — they provide situational awareness but do not prohibit operations. However, dispatchers and PICs must assess the risk per their airline's safety management system (SMS) and 14 CFR §121.533 operational control requirements. If the bird activity creates a specific hazard level (e.g., migratory flock at departure runway threshold), the PIC has authority and responsibility to delay or divert under 14 CFR §91.3.
required_safety_constraints: Bird activity NOTAMs are advisory, not operational prohibitions
The PIC retains final authority under 14 CFR §91.3 even for advisory NOTAMs when safety is at risk
Part 121 dispatchers share operational control and must assess bird strike risk per SMS procedures (14 CFR §121.533)
Advisory NOTAMs should not be dismissed — they can elevate to safety-critical status depending on conditions
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-1 — Advisory vs mandatory NOTAM classification

provenance.generation_rule: Bird activity NOTAM is a standard FAA NOTAM category per JO 7930.2S. Advisory/mandatory distinction is a core Type A knowledge probe. Regulatory citations 14 CFR §91.3 and §121.533 are standard US aviation law.

prompt: What are the five Q-line traffic type codes and which traffic types does a NOTAM apply to when the traffic field reads 'IV'?
gold_decision: The five traffic type codes in the NOTAM Q-line are: I=IFR, V=VFR, K=Checklist (NOTAM list maintenance), IV=IFR and VFR, IK=IFR and Checklist. Traffic code IV means the NOTAM applies to both IFR and VFR operations. All IFR and VFR pilots and dispatchers operating in the affected area must include this NOTAM in their briefing and planning.
required_safety_constraints: Traffic type IV is the most common code — it applies to both IFR and VFR flights
Traffic type K is for NOTAM checklist maintenance only and does not constitute operational notice to pilots
A NOTAM with traffic type I does not apply to VFR-only operations and vice versa
evidence_requirements: ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 §6.2.4 — Traffic type codes
FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix A — Q-line traffic field

provenance.generation_rule: Traffic type codes I/V/K/IV/IK from ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 §6.2.4 and FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix A. IV is the most operationally significant code — correct identification is a basic NOTAM literacy item.

prompt:
A NOTAM A-field lists two locations: A) KJFK KEWR

To which airports does this NOTAM apply and how must it be treated for pre-flight planning?
gold_decision: The NOTAM applies to both KJFK (John F. Kennedy) and KEWR (Newark Liberty). The A-field can contain multiple ICAO location indicators, meaning the same condition affects all listed airports. Pre-flight planning for any flight into, out of, or over either airport must include this NOTAM. Dispatchers must include it in both the KJFK and KEWR NOTAM packages.
required_safety_constraints: Multiple A-field locations means the NOTAM is simultaneously applicable to all listed airports
Do not assume a multi-airport NOTAM only applies to the first listed location
Each listed airport must independently acknowledge applicability in the dispatch release
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S §3-3-3 — A-field with multiple locations
ICAO Annex 15 §6.2.7 — A-field multiple location indicator format

provenance.generation_rule: Multiple-location A-field format per FAA JO 7930.2S §3-3-3. KJFK and KEWR used as representative New York area airports — both are real ICAO identifiers within FIR KZNY.

prompt:
The E-field of a NOTAM reads: 'TWY ALPHA BTN TWY BRAVO AND TWY CHARLIE CLSD'

Which taxiway segment is closed and what taxiway identifiers are referenced?
gold_decision: Taxiway Alpha is closed between its intersection with Taxiway Bravo and its intersection with Taxiway Charlie. The referenced taxiway identifiers are TWY A (Alpha), TWY B (Bravo), and TWY C (Charlie). Aircraft must not use the segment of Taxiway Alpha between these two intersections. Alternative taxi routing must be obtained from ground control.
required_safety_constraints: Taxiway closures are mandatory — aircraft must not enter a closed taxiway segment without ATC authorization
BTN = Between in FAA NOTAM contractions (JO 7930.2S Appendix B)
The closure applies specifically to the segment between the named intersections, not the entire taxiway
Pilots must request alternate taxi routing from ground control when taxiway closure affects planned route
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix B — NOTAM contractions (BTN, TWY, CLSD)
ICAO Annex 15 — Aerodrome movement area NOTAM conventions

provenance.generation_rule: NOTAM E-field text constructed using standard FAA contractions BTN (between), TWY (taxiway), CLSD (closed) per JO 7930.2S Appendix B. Taxiway segment closure is a common airport surface NOTAM type.

prompt: What is the NOTAM F-field and G-field used for, and which airspace operations are affected by them?
gold_decision: The F-field specifies the lower vertical limit and the G-field specifies the upper vertical limit of the airspace affected by the NOTAM. They are used for en-route or airspace-related NOTAMs (e.g., temporary restricted areas, navigation hazards at altitude). Aircraft operating at altitudes within the F-G range in the affected area are subject to the NOTAM. Aircraft entirely above or below the stated range are not directly affected by altitude-limited NOTAMs.
required_safety_constraints: F/G fields are only present when the NOTAM has a vertical extent — absence means the NOTAM is surface-level or affects the entire usable altitude range
F/G limits are expressed in feet MSL or flight levels as specified
A NOTAM with no F/G field but scope W (warning) applies to all altitudes in the affected area
evidence_requirements: ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 §6.2.8 — F and G fields (lower/upper limits)
FAA JO 7930.2S §3-3-7 — Lower and upper limits

provenance.generation_rule: F/G field definitions directly from ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 §6.2.8 and FAA JO 7930.2S §3-3-7. F/G applicability for en-route altitude filtering is a Type A knowledge question.

prompt: A NOTAM Q-line contains F) 050 G) 200. A flight is cruising at FL180. Is this NOTAM relevant to the flight?
gold_decision: No. The NOTAM lower limit is FL050 (5,000 ft) and upper limit is FL200 (20,000 ft). However, FL180 falls within the FL050-FL200 band, so the NOTAM IS relevant. Correction: FL180 is between FL050 and FL200, so the NOTAM applies to this flight. The crew must be aware of the restriction during the portion of flight at FL180 that transits the geographic area defined by the Q-line coordinates.
required_safety_constraints: NOTAM vertical applicability: FL050 to FL200 inclusive means any aircraft at FL050-FL199 (or FL200 depending on inclusive/exclusive convention) must comply
FL180 falls within FL050-FL200 range — NOTAM is operationally relevant
Always verify whether stated FL limits are inclusive or exclusive at the boundary
acceptable_variants: Some operators use exclusive upper bound interpretation (FL200 means up to but not including FL200) — check airline ops specs
evidence_requirements: ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 §6.2.8 — F and G fields

provenance.generation_rule: Altitude applicability test constructed from ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 F/G field convention. FL050/FL200 chosen to create a clear boundary test case. FL180 deliberately placed within the range to probe whether agents correctly evaluate range membership.

prompt: A pilot receives a pre-flight NOTAM briefing at 09:00 UTC. The flight departs at 14:00 UTC. A NOTAM is issued at 11:30 UTC affecting the destination airport. Is the pilot legally required to obtain updated NOTAM information before departure?
gold_decision: Yes. Under 14 CFR §91.103, the PIC must obtain all available information concerning the flight before departure. Although the original briefing was complete at 09:00 UTC, a NOTAM issued at 11:30 UTC is 'available information' that was not in the original briefing. The PIC must obtain an updated NOTAM briefing before the 14:00 UTC departure. Failure to do so may constitute non-compliance with §91.103.
required_safety_constraints: 14 CFR §91.103 requires the PIC to obtain all available information — this includes NOTAMs issued after the initial briefing
Pre-flight briefings are time-stamped; a briefing does not remain valid indefinitely
NOTAMs issued after the briefing and before departure must be checked (industry best practice: recheck within 1 hour of departure)
Part 121 dispatchers must provide updated NOTAM information to the PIC when operationally significant NOTAMs are issued (14 CFR §121.601)
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §121.601 — Aircraft dispatcher information to pilot in command

provenance.generation_rule: Knowledge point from 14 CFR §91.103 preflight briefing currency requirement. Scenario designed to probe understanding that pre-flight NOTAM checks have a time dimension — a common operational misconception.

prompt: What does NOTAM contraction 'AP' stand for, and where is it defined?
gold_decision: AP is the FAA NOTAM contraction for 'Airport'. It is defined in FAA Order JO 7930.2S Appendix B (NOTAM Contractions). Example usage: 'AP CLSD' = Airport Closed. ICAO uses the acronym AD (Aerodrome) in international NOTAMs per ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6.
required_safety_constraints: FAA contractions differ from ICAO abbreviations — AP (FAA) = AD (ICAO) for aerodrome/airport
Using the wrong contraction system can lead to misinterpretation of NOTAM text in international operations
acceptable_variants: AD is the ICAO equivalent; both should be recognized in international operations
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix B — NOTAM Contractions (AP)

provenance.generation_rule: NOTAM contraction AP from FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix B. ICAO equivalent AD from ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 2 abbreviation list. Tests basic NOTAM contraction literacy.

prompt:
A NOTAM is issued with the Q-line: Q) EGTT/QNVAS/IV/NBO/AE/000/999/5130N00007W050

The subject code NV indicates 'Navigation — ATIS'. The condition code AS indicates 'Unserviceable'. What operational impact does this NOTAM have?
gold_decision: The NOTAM indicates that the ATIS (Automatic Terminal Information Service) at the affected aerodrome is unserviceable. Operationally: crews cannot obtain automated weather/runway/NOTAM information via ATIS. They must obtain equivalent information by contacting ATC directly (ATIS frequency becomes unavailable). The scope AE means this affects both aerodrome arrivals/departures and may affect pilots planning en-route transitions through the area. Pilots must request current information from approach/departure control or tower.
required_safety_constraints: ATIS U/S means pre-departure and arrival weather/runway info must be obtained directly from ATC
Operations to airports without functioning ATIS require additional coordination time — build into planning
QNVAS is a standard Q-code: NV=Navigation, AS=unserviceable per ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6
evidence_requirements: ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 — Q-code table NV (Navigation/ATIS)

provenance.generation_rule: Q-code QNVAS constructed from ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 Q-code table: Q+NV(Navigation/ATIS)+AS(unserviceable). EGTT=London AC FIR. Coordinates notional London area. NBO purpose code = Normal, Briefing, Flight Operations. Tests Q-code decoding + operational implication reasoning.

prompt: Under ICAO Annex 15, what is the maximum total character length of a NOTAM E-field (plain-language text), and what must be done if information exceeds this limit?
gold_decision: Under ICAO Annex 15, the E-field plain-language text is limited to 2000 characters (standard AFTN message size constraints apply). When information exceeds this limit, the NOTAM must be split into multiple NOTAMs or a SNOWFLAKE/AIXM encoded message format may be used. In FAA practice under JO 7930.2S, the E-field should be as concise as possible using approved contractions to stay within the character limit.
required_safety_constraints: Truncated NOTAMs due to character limits are a known data quality risk — operators must check for split NOTAMs with similar series numbers and time windows
SNOWFLAKE/digital NOTAM formats remove this constraint but may not be universally supported by older crew briefing systems
acceptable_variants: Exact character limit varies by distribution format; 2000 chars is the AFTN standard; digital NOTAM (ICAO Doc 10066 NOTAM format) uses XML with no hard character limit
evidence_requirements: ICAO Doc 8126 §4.5 — AFTN message format constraints

provenance.generation_rule: Character limit knowledge point from ICAO Annex 15 §6.2.9 and ICAO Doc 8126 AFTN constraints. Tests awareness of NOTAM format limitations that can cause data quality issues in AI processing.

prompt:
A NOTAM E-field reads: 'OBST CRANE (ASN 2025-AWP-14562-OE) 1NM NNW KBUR ELEV 1285FT (350FT AGL) FLAGGED AND LIGHTED.'

What Q-line subject code applies to this NOTAM, and what type of aircraft operations are affected?
gold_decision: The Q-line subject code for an obstacle (crane) NOTAM is QO (Obstacle). The full Q-code would typically be QOBCE (Obstacle, Crane, Erected) or similar. Operations affected: IFR and VFR departures and arrivals at KBUR (Bob Hope Airport, Burbank, CA) within the terminal area. Aircraft conducting instrument approaches or VFR pattern work within 1NM NNW of KBUR at altitudes below 1285 ft MSL must be aware of this obstacle. The obstacle is flagged and lighted, which partially mitigates night VFR risk.
required_safety_constraints: ASN (Airspace Safety Number) is the FAA identification number for an obstacle determination (from FAA 7460-1 filing)
Obstacle NOTAMs within 1NM of an airport threshold require crew awareness for all approach categories
ELEV = obstacle elevation above MSL; AGL = above ground level — both values are provided per JO 7930.2S Appendix B
Flagged and lighted = the obstruction is marked per 14 CFR Part 77 lighting requirements
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix B — OBST, ELEV, AGL contractions
ICAO Annex 15 Appendix 6 — QO subject codes (obstacles)
14 CFR Part 77 — Objects affecting navigable airspace

provenance.generation_rule: Obstacle NOTAM format from FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix B. ASN format per FAA 7460-1 OE determination process. KBUR airport used as real identifier. Elevation 1285 ft MSL / 350 ft AGL consistent with Burbank area terrain. ASN number is notional.

prompt: What is the operational significance of the NOTAM contraction 'ACT' versus 'AVBL' in a NOTAM E-field?
gold_decision: ACT means Active — the condition or facility is currently active and in effect. AVBL means Available — the facility or service is available for use but may not be continuously active. For example, 'GPS RNAV AVBL' indicates GPS RNAV is available. 'TEMPO RESTRICTED AREA ACT' indicates the temporary restriction is currently active. Operationally, an ACT restriction requires immediate compliance; an AVBL facility note means the service can be used but may require activation by ATC or may not be continuously broadcast.
required_safety_constraints: ACT = currently active operational condition requiring compliance
AVBL = available but not necessarily active — verify with ATC before relying on the service
Confusing AVBL with ACT can lead to assuming a restriction is active when it is only pre-filed, or assuming a navaid is transmitting when it requires activation
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix B — NOTAM contractions ACT and AVBL

provenance.generation_rule: Contractions ACT and AVBL from FAA JO 7930.2S Appendix B. The ACT/AVBL distinction is a core disambiguation test for NOTAM literacy.

prompt: A NOTAM series letter is 'D'. What type of NOTAM does the D-series designate in the FAA NOTAM system?
gold_decision: In the FAA NOTAM system, D-series NOTAMs (D-NOTAMs) are Distant NOTAMs that are disseminated via the National NOTAM System (NNS) and are available to all users through standard pilot briefing services. They cover information that is not local in nature — e.g., en-route navaids, airspace, GPS outages. This contrasts with L-NOTAMs (Local NOTAMs) which are maintained locally and may not appear in standard preflight briefings. FDC NOTAMs (Flight Data Center) cover regulatory or procedural changes such as instrument procedure amendments.
required_safety_constraints: L-NOTAMs are NOT in the standard DUATS/ATC briefing system — pilots must obtain them from the local airport/FSS directly
FDC NOTAMs take precedence for instrument procedure changes and must be checked in addition to D-NOTAMs
All NOTAM types must be checked for a complete pre-flight briefing

provenance.generation_rule: D-NOTAM classification from FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-2. Tests awareness of the multi-tier FAA NOTAM dissemination system — a common source of missed NOTAMs.

prompt: Under ICAO Annex 15 §4.1, within what timeframe must a NOTAM be originated after the aeronautical information it describes becomes available?
gold_decision: Under ICAO Annex 15 §4.1, a NOTAM must be originated as soon as practicable after the relevant information becomes available. For planned changes, NOTAMs should be issued at least 7 days in advance where possible, or by AIRAC cycle (56-day or 28-day advance notice) for permanent changes. For unplanned events (e.g., runway lights failure), NOTAMs must be issued immediately — within minutes for safety-critical information.
required_safety_constraints: Safety-critical NOTAM information must be issued immediately — no delay is acceptable for active hazards
AIRAC cycle advance publication (56/28 days) applies to planned aeronautical changes per ICAO Annex 15 §6.1
Delays in NOTAM origination are themselves a safety deficiency reportable under SMS procedures
evidence_requirements: ICAO Annex 15 §4.4 — AIRAC cycle publication requirements

provenance.generation_rule: Origination timeliness requirement from ICAO Annex 15 §4.1. AIRAC cycle reference from §4.4. Tests understanding of NOTAM currency as a data quality dimension.

prompt: A NOTAM is labeled 'NOTAMR A0456/26'. What does the R designator confirm about NOTAM A0456/26, and what must the dispatcher check?
gold_decision: The R designator means this is a Replacement NOTAM. NOTAM A0456/26 has been superseded by this new NOTAM. The dispatcher must: (1) remove A0456/26 from the active NOTAM package; (2) check the E-field of the replacement NOTAM to understand what has changed; (3) verify whether the effective times (B/C fields) have changed; (4) verify the scope/subject/condition in the Q-line has changed. Failure to update the NOTAM package with the replacement constitutes use of stale information.
required_safety_constraints: A NOTAMR explicitly supersedes the referenced NOTAM — treating the old NOTAM as still valid is a regulatory and safety violation
The dispatcher must ensure crew briefing materials are updated to reflect the replacement
NOTAMR may extend, shorten, or modify the condition in the original NOTAM — all fields must be re-read
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §121.601 — Dispatcher responsibilities for current information

provenance.generation_rule: NOTAM R-type from FAA JO 7930.2S §2-1-3. NOTAM number A0456/26 uses real format (series A, number 0456, year 26). Tests dispatcher action on NOTAMR receipt.

prompt: What Q-code indicates a GPS RAIM prediction outage, and what is the implication for RNAV approaches?
gold_decision: The Q-code for GPS outage/RAIM prediction failures is QGPXX (GPS, various conditions) or more specifically QGNSS/QGPS (varies by FIR). In FAA practice, GPS NOTAMs are issued in the FDC category and may also use a GPS-specific format. A RAIM prediction outage NOTAM means that GPS integrity monitoring is predicted to be unavailable during the specified window. For RNAV approaches requiring GPS (LP, LPV, LNAV/VNAV), the approach may not be legal or safe during the outage window. Pilots must have a non-GPS instrument approach or alternate airport available.
required_safety_constraints: RAIM outage means GPS integrity cannot be assured — RNAV approaches requiring GPS integrity are not permitted
LP and LPV approaches require RAIM or equivalent SBAS integrity monitoring
LNAV/VNAV requires baro-VNAV if GPS VNAV is unavailable due to RAIM outage
Dispatchers must ensure alternate approach capability exists when GPS RAIM NOTAMs are issued for destination or alternate airports

provenance.generation_rule: GPS RAIM NOTAM knowledge from FAA AIM §1-1-13 and FAA JO 7930.2S GPS NOTAM section. Q-code prefix QGPS/QGNSS from ICAO Annex 15 Q-code table for navigation systems. Tests critical GPS-NOTAM applicability chain.

prompt: What is the NOTAM Q-code for a temporary flight restriction (TFR), and what authority issues TFRs in US airspace?
gold_decision: TFRs in US airspace are issued via NOTAM by the FAA Flight Data Center (FDC NOTAM). The Q-code typically used is QRTCA (Restricted/TFR/Activity Area created) or a variant. TFRs are issued under 14 CFR §91.137 (disaster/hazard), §91.138 (national disaster areas), §91.141 (presidential/VIP), §91.143 (space operations), and §91.145 (major events). Violating a TFR is a serious violation potentially triggering military intercept and certificate action.
required_safety_constraints: TFR violations are treated as security incidents by ATC/military — strict compliance required
FDC NOTAMs must be checked in addition to D-NOTAMs to capture TFRs
TFRs under §91.141 (presidential) have no hardcoded radius — always check the specific NOTAM text for dimensions
Even VFR flights must comply with TFRs unless explicitly exempted in the NOTAM text
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S — FDC NOTAM and TFR format

provenance.generation_rule: TFR regulatory basis 14 CFR §91.137-§91.145. FDC NOTAM format per FAA JO 7930.2S. Q-code QRTCA from ICAO Annex 15 restricted area subject. Tests knowledge of TFR authority and severity.

prompt: Define SNOWFLAKE in the context of NOTAM distribution and explain how it differs from traditional AFTN-based NOTAM distribution.
gold_decision: SNOWFLAKE is the ICAO name for the digital NOTAM exchange system that uses AIXM (Aeronautical Information Exchange Model) 5.1 XML-based data format rather than the traditional plain-text AFTN (Aeronautical Fixed Telecommunication Network) format. Key differences: (1) SNOWFLAKE removes the 2000-character AFTN message limit; (2) AIXM enables machine-readable geospatial data with structured fields; (3) supports direct database updates rather than text parsing; (4) enables more precise validity period and affected area specification. Traditional AFTN NOTAMs require text parsing which is error-prone for AI and automated systems.
required_safety_constraints: Not all briefing systems support SNOWFLAKE/digital NOTAMs — confirm support before assuming digital distribution is complete
AI systems trained on AFTN-format NOTAMs may not handle AIXM format correctly without adaptation
evidence_requirements: ICAO Doc 10066 — NOTAM format (digital NOTAM / SNOWFLAKE specification)

provenance.generation_rule: SNOWFLAKE and AIXM 5.1 reference from ICAO Doc 10066 and Annex 15 §6.3. Tests awareness of modern digital NOTAM formats vs legacy AFTN text NOTAMs — relevant for AI system design context.

prompt: A runway has both a published ILS and an RNAV (GPS) approach. NOTAM states: 'ILS RWY 10L GS U/S'. Can aircraft still fly the ILS LOC approach to RWY 10L?
gold_decision: Yes. When only the Glideslope (GS) is unserviceable, a LOC-only approach (ILS Localizer approach) is still possible if published. The LOC-only approach has higher minimums (MDA instead of DA) than the full ILS. The aircraft can track the localizer inbound but must use the LOC-only minimums published on the instrument approach chart. The RNAV (GPS) approach is completely unaffected and also remains available.
required_safety_constraints: ILS GS U/S means full ILS approach is unavailable, but LOC-only approach is available if published with separate minimums
LOC-only minimums are typically 200-300 ft higher than ILS DA — check the approach chart for published LOC minimums
RNAV (GPS) approach is independent of ILS system status and remains fully available
Crew must brief the LOC-only approach including the higher MDA before commencing the approach
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2S — ILS GS NOTAM interpretation

provenance.generation_rule: GS U/S NOTAM scenario per FAA JO 7930.2S GS unserviceable NOTAM format. LOC-only vs full ILS distinction per 14 CFR §91.175 and FAA AIM §5-4-5. Tests understanding of partial ILS component unserviceability.
